What to Do When You Get in a Traffic Accident? — Facing the Aftermath (2)
In the previous post we covered what to do at the scene. This post covers things that come up after — things you might not have considered but are really important.

I'm not a professional. This post is based on my own situation and what I learned along the way. If there's anything off, feel free to correct me. (Also note: this is based on the legal context in Taiwan.)

I put this at the top because — in my case — I only notified them the day after the accident, since I only had mandatory insurance. If you have third-party liability insurance, I'd recommend notifying them right after you call 119.
Notify your insurance company within five days of the accident.

Whether you have third-party or just mandatory insurance, you need to notify them within five days, otherwise your rights may be impacted (i.e., the insurance company may refuse to pay out). Whether you're a driver, passenger, or pedestrian, this applies.

In my experience, the process is super simple — just call your insurance company and tell them you had an accident. They'll note your info (that's it for mandatory insurance). For third-party, the insurance company will send someone to the scene to help.
For third-party, an insurance adjuster comes to the scene, takes photos, surveys the scene, handles the compensation logistics — so you don't have to worry about what's next.
Reach out to the other party

Regardless of fault, I think this matters. When someone gets injured, it's not a simple thing — so I'd recommend reaching out and asking if there's anything the other party needs help with. They'll feel your goodwill, and might even reach out to you in return.
"Reaching out is basic decency — but don't randomly admit fault. Especially before the evidence is in, never just admit fault."
Does reaching out actually help close out the situation faster? Honestly, hard to say — everyone handles things differently. But reaching out is a way to show good faith and intent to resolve.
Whether the other party will forgive you is a separate issue — it involves people, which is always more complex. But I think reaching out matters.
For every call, I'd recommend screenshotting or recording it. Those records can be presented in court — whether or not a judge admits them is another matter.
In my case, both sides were injured, so we needed ongoing medical visits. Keep all your medical records — you'll need the receipts when claiming against the other party's insurance.
Also: you can go back to the scene to take more photos to gather evidence.
By now you should have or have done these:
Other party's contact info and call records
A reminder: don't apply for the formal medical diagnosis certificate before mediation. The insurance company and court will only look at the final diagnosis, so no need to rush.
Traffic accidents can become really complicated — like going to court — so you'll want to talk to legal professionals for advice.
Where to find help? There are a number of free legal consultation services:
Online legal consultation (some offer free Line consultations)
For me, I consulted at township/district offices, constituent service offices, and online legal consultations.

Heads up — free legal consultations usually have limits: only one question, time-capped 30 minutes to 1 hour. So organize your questions in advance. And it gets really crowded — in my case I waited 2 hours before my turn.

Honest take: free legal consultation gives you a direction so you're less lost, but because their time is limited and they're serving many people, they can't go deep on your case. They'll just answer basic legal questions without follow-up support — they're free aid after all.
Pros and cons of free legal consultation:
Can't go deep on the case
The person responding may not specialize in your area
Some people exaggerate (I've experienced this — they exaggerate because they want you to retain them)

A lot of cons — does that mean free legal consultation is useless? I don't think so. The premise is to provide some basic legal answers for free. If you click with the person handling your consultation, you can consider retaining them — but that's your call.
I'd recommend consulting at multiple places — that way you get a range of opinions and can compare. But be cautious — too many opinions can also make you more confused.

If you have a special status (e.g., disadvantaged group), you can go straight to the Legal Aid Foundation. They can pair you with a free legal aid lawyer, but you'll need to provide more identity documents.

One more thing — if you're hospitalized, sometimes people will come up and ask what happened, because they're lawyers looking for cases. They'll do an initial assessment for free to see if they can win, and many will take cases on contingency — taking a cut of the eventual compensation as their fee.

This is the most important one. Accidents make people anxious, and the whole process is draining. I'd recommend finding ways to manage your mental state — go out, talk with friends, watch a movie, etc. Or find a friend to vent to as your emotional outlet.
You need to get your headspace right to handle this stuff. Trying to avoid it just delays the inevitable.
Don't try to escape — the thing happened, you have to face it. But you can choose what mental state to face it in.

Basically that's it. Next up is waiting for the "preliminary report" (Preliminary Traffic Accident Analysis Report) to come out. In the next post I'll cover the application process for everything that follows.
